| * [Ancient](/wiki/Ancient_Olympic_Games "Ancient Olympic Games") * [Esports Series](/wiki/Olympic_Esports_Series "Olympic Esports Series") * [Intercalated](/wiki/1906_Intercalated_Games#Intercalated_Games "1906 Intercalated Games")

(French: *Jeux olympiques d'hiver*),[[a]](#cite_note-2) also known as the **Olympic Winter Games** or simply the **Winter Olympics**, is a major international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") held once every four years for sports practiced on snow and ice. The Winter Olympics are usually held in February, during the winter season of the Northern Hemisphere. The IOC is the governing body of the [Olympic Movement](/wiki/Olympic_movement "Olympic movement"), with the [Olympic Charter](/wiki/Olympic_Charter "Olympic Charter") defining its structure and authority. [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") and [Cortina d'Ampezzo](/wiki/Cortina_d%27Ampezzo "Cortina d'Ampezzo") of Italy hosted in 2026. The next games are scheduled to be held between 1 to 17 February 2030 at the [French Alps](/wiki/2030_Winter_Olympics "2030 Winter Olympics").[[2]](#cite_note-3)[[3]](#cite_note-4)

The **Winter Olympic Games** (French: *Jeux olympiques d'hiver*),[[a]](#cite_note-2) also known as the **Olympic Winter Games** or simply the **Winter Olympics**, is a major international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") held once every four years for sports practiced on snow and ice. The Winter Olympics are usually held in February, during the winter season of the Northern Hemisphere. The IOC is the governing body of the [Olympic Movement](/wiki/Olympic_movement "Olympic movement"), with the [Olympic Charter](/wiki/Olympic_Charter "Olympic Charter") defining its structure and authority. [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") and [Cortina d'Ampezzo](/wiki/Cortina_d%27Ampezzo "Cortina d'Ampezzo") of Italy hosted in 2026. The next games are scheduled to be held between 1 to 17 February 2030 at the [French Alps](/wiki/2030_Winter_Olympics "2030 Winter Olympics").[[2]](#cite_note-3)[[3]](#cite_note-4)

The original five Winter Olympic Sports (consisting of nine disciplines) were [bobsleigh](/wiki/Bobsleigh_at_the_1924_Winter_Olympics "Bobsleigh at the 1924 Winter Olympics"), [curling](/wiki/Curling_at_the_1924_Winter_Olympics "Curling at the 1924 Winter Olympics"), [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1924_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1924 Winter Olympics"), [Nordic skiing](/wiki/Nordic_skiing_at_the_1924_Winter_Olympics "Nordic skiing at the 1924 Winter Olympics") (consisting of the disciplines [military patrol](/wiki/Military_patrol_at_the_1924_Winter_Olympics "Military patrol at the 1924 Winter Olympics"),[[b]](#cite_note-IOCMilitaryPatrol-5) [cross-country skiing](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_1924_Winter_Olympics "Cross-country skiing at the 1924 Winter Olympics"), [Nordic combined](/wiki/Nordic_combined_at_the_1924_Winter_Olympics "Nordic combined at the 1924 Winter Olympics"), and [ski jumping](/wiki/Ski_jumping_at_the_1924_Winter_Olympics "Ski jumping at the 1924 Winter Olympics")), and [skating](/wiki/Ice_Skating "Ice Skating") (consisting of the disciplines [figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1924_Winter_Olympics "Figure skating at the 1924 Winter Olympics") and [speed skating](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1924_Winter_Olympics "Speed skating at the 1924 Winter Olympics")).[[c]](#cite_note-6) The Winter Olympic Games have evolved since their inception. Sports and disciplines have been added and some of them, such as [alpine skiing](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Alpine skiing at the Winter Olympics"), [luge](/wiki/Luge_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Luge at the Winter Olympics"), [short track speed skating](/wiki/Short_track_speed_skating_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Short track speed skating at the Winter Olympics"), [freestyle skiing](/wiki/Freestyle_skiing_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Freestyle skiing at the Winter Olympics"), [skeleton](/wiki/Skeleton_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Skeleton at the Winter Olympics"), and [snowboarding](/wiki/Snowboarding_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Snowboarding at the Winter Olympics"), have earned a permanent spot on the Olympic program. Some others, including [curling](/wiki/Curling_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Curling at the Winter Olympics") and [bobsleigh](/wiki/Bobsleigh_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Bobsleigh at the Winter Olympics"), have been discontinued and later reintroduced; others have been permanently discontinued, such as [military patrol](/wiki/Military_patrol_(sport) "Military patrol (sport)"), though the modern Winter Olympic sport of [biathlon](/wiki/Biathlon_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Biathlon at the Winter Olympics") is descended from it.[[b]](#cite_note-IOCMilitaryPatrol-5) Still others, such as [speed skiing](/wiki/Speed_skiing "Speed skiing"), [bandy](/wiki/Bandy "Bandy") and [skijoring](/wiki/Skijoring "Skijoring"), were [demonstration sports](/wiki/Demonstration_sports "Demonstration sports") but never incorporated as Olympic sports.

The first Winter Olympic Games, the [1924 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1924_Winter_Olympics "1924 Winter Olympics"), were held in [Chamonix](/wiki/Chamonix "Chamonix"), France. The modern Olympic Games were inspired by the [ancient Olympic Games](/wiki/Ancient_Olympic_Games "Ancient Olympic Games"), which were held in [Olympia, Greece](/wiki/Olympia,_Greece "Olympia, Greece"), from 776 BCE to 394 CE. The [Baron Pierre de Coubertin](/wiki/Pierre_de_Coubertin "Pierre de Coubertin") of France founded the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") (IOC) 1,500 years later in 1894, leading to the first modern [Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games") in Athens, Greece in 1896. The Games were held every four years from 1924 to 1936, interrupted in 1940 and 1944 by [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), and resumed in [1948](/wiki/1948_Winter_Olympics "1948 Winter Olympics"). Until 1992, the [Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games") and the Winter Olympic Games were held in the same year. A decision to change this was made in 1986, when during the [91st International Olympic Committee session](/wiki/List_of_IOC_meetings "List of IOC meetings"), IOC members decided to alternate the Summer Olympic Games and the Winter Olympic Games on separate four-year cycles in even-numbered years. Also, at that same congress it was decided that [1992 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1992_Winter_Olympics "1992 Winter Olympics") would be the last to be held in the same year as the Summer Games and that to change the rotation, the games that would be held in 1996 would be brought forward by two years, being scheduled to [1994](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics "1994 Winter Olympics"), the same four-year cycle as the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"), and the men's [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup"). After those games, the next were to be [held in 1998](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics") when the four-year Olympic Cycle resumed.[[8]](#cite_note-11)[[9]](#cite_note-12)

The Winter Olympic Games have been hosted on three continents by thirteen countries, all of whom are located in the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere"). They have been held four times in the [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_Olympics "United States at the Olympics") ([1932](/wiki/1932_Winter_Olympics "1932 Winter Olympics"), [1960](/wiki/1960_Winter_Olympics "1960 Winter Olympics"), [1980](/wiki/1980_Winter_Olympics "1980 Winter Olympics"), and [2002](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics")), three times each in [France](/wiki/France_at_the_Olympics "France at the Olympics") ([1924](/wiki/1924_Winter_Olympics "1924 Winter Olympics"), [1968](/wiki/1968_Winter_Olympics "1968 Winter Olympics"), and [1992](/wiki/1992_Winter_Olympics "1992 Winter Olympics")) and [Italy](/wiki/Italy_at_the_Olympics "Italy at the Olympics") ([1956](/wiki/1956_Winter_Olympics "1956 Winter Olympics"), [2006](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics"), and [2026](/wiki/2026_Winter_Olympics "2026 Winter Olympics")), and twice each in [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_Olympics "Switzerland at the Olympics") ([1928](/wiki/1928_Winter_Olympics "1928 Winter Olympics") and [1948](/wiki/1948_Winter_Olympics "1948 Winter Olympics")), [Austria](/wiki/Austria_at_the_Olympics "Austria at the Olympics") ([1964](/wiki/1964_Winter_Olympics "1964 Winter Olympics") and [1976](/wiki/1976_Winter_Olympics "1976 Winter Olympics")), [Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_Olympics "Norway at the Olympics") ([1952](/wiki/1952_Winter_Olympics "1952 Winter Olympics") and [1994](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics "1994 Winter Olympics")), [Japan](/wiki/Japan_at_the_Olympics "Japan at the Olympics") ([1972](/wiki/1972_Winter_Olympics "1972 Winter Olympics") and [1998](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics")) and [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_Olympics "Canada at the Olympics") ([1988](/wiki/1988_Winter_Olympics "1988 Winter Olympics") and [2010](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics")). Also, the Winter Olympic Games have been held once each in [Germany](/wiki/Germany_at_the_Olympics "Germany at the Olympics") ([1936](/wiki/1936_Winter_Olympics "1936 Winter Olympics")), [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia_at_the_Olympics "Yugoslavia at the Olympics") ([1984](/wiki/1984_Winter_Olympics "1984 Winter Olympics")), [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_Olympics "Russia at the Olympics") ([2014](/wiki/2014_Winter_Olympics "2014 Winter Olympics")), [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_at_the_Olympics "South Korea at the Olympics") ([2018](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics "2018 Winter Olympics")), and [China](/wiki/China_at_the_Olympics "China at the Olympics") ([2022](/wiki/2022_Winter_Olympics "2022 Winter Olympics")).

As of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Winter_Olympic_Games&action=edit), twelve countries have participated in every Winter Olympic Games – [Austria](/wiki/Austria_at_the_Olympics "Austria at the Olympics"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the Winter Olympics"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_Olympics "Finland at the Olympics"), [France](/wiki/France_at_the_Olympics "France at the Olympics"), [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain_at_the_Olympics "Great Britain at the Olympics"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary_at_the_Olympics "Hungary at the Olympics"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy_at_the_Olympics "Italy at the Olympics"), [Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_Olympics "Norway at the Olympics"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland_at_the_Olympics "Poland at the Olympics"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_Olympics "Sweden at the Olympics"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_Olympics "Switzerland at the Olympics"), and the [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_Olympics "United States at the Olympics"). Also, [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia_at_the_Olympics "Czechoslovakia at the Olympics") participated in all Winter Olympic Games before its [dissolution](/wiki/Dissolution_of_Czechoslovakia "Dissolution of Czechoslovakia") and its successors, [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_at_the_Olympics "Czech Republic at the Olympics") and [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_at_the_Olympics "Slovakia at the Olympics") have participated in all Winter Games thereafter. Six of these countries have won medals at every Winter Olympic Games – Austria, Canada, Finland, Norway, Sweden, and the United States. The only country to have won a gold medal at every Winter Olympic Games is the United States.

Norway leads the [all-time medal record](/wiki/All-time_Olympic_Games_medal_table "All-time Olympic Games medal table") for the Winter Olympic Games, followed by the United States and Germany. When including defunct states, Germany (including the former countries of West Germany and East Germany) leads, followed by Norway, Russia (including the former Soviet Union) and the United States. Norway holds the record for most gold medals in any one Winter Olympics, with 18 in 2026, and overall medals, with 41 in 2026.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Winter_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Winter_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early years")]

[![a man standing on ice in figure skates](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/1908_Olympic_Games_Ulrich_Salchow.jpg/250px-1908_Olympic_Games_Ulrich_Salchow.jpg)](/wiki/File:1908_Olympic_Games_Ulrich_Salchow.jpg)

[Ulrich Salchow](/wiki/Ulrich_Salchow "Ulrich Salchow") at the 1908 Olympics

A predecessor, the [Nordic Games](/wiki/Nordic_Games "Nordic Games"), were organised by General [Viktor Gustaf Balck](/wiki/Viktor_Gustaf_Balck "Viktor Gustaf Balck") in [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), Sweden, in 1901 and were held again in 1903 and 1905 and then every fourth year thereafter until 1926.[[10]](#cite_note-origins-13) Balck was a charter member of the [IOC](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") and a close friend of [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") founder [Pierre de Coubertin](/wiki/Pierre_de_Coubertin "Pierre de Coubertin"). He attempted to have winter sports, specifically [figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating "Figure skating"), added to the Olympic programme but was unsuccessful until the [1908 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1908_Summer_Olympics "1908 Summer Olympics") in London.[[10]](#cite_note-origins-13) Four figure skating events were contested, at which [Ulrich Salchow](/wiki/Ulrich_Salchow "Ulrich Salchow") (10-time world champion) and [Madge Syers](/wiki/Madge_Syers "Madge Syers") won the individual titles.[[11]](#cite_note-14)[[12]](#cite_note-15)

Three years later, Italian count [Eugenio Brunetta d'Usseaux](/wiki/Eugenio_Brunetta_d%27Usseaux "Eugenio Brunetta d'Usseaux") proposed that the IOC stage a week of winter sports included as part of the [1912 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1912_Summer_Olympics "1912 Summer Olympics") in Stockholm, Sweden. The organisers opposed this idea because they desired to protect the integrity of the Nordic Games and were concerned about a lack of facilities for winter sports.[[13]](#cite_note-16)[[14]](#cite_note-cbc.ca-17)[[15]](#cite_note-18)

The idea was resurrected for the [1916 Games](/wiki/1916_Summer_Olympics "1916 Summer Olympics"), which were to be held in Berlin, Germany. A [winter sports week](/wiki/International_Winter_Sports_Week "International Winter Sports Week") with [speed skating](/wiki/Speed_skating "Speed skating"), figure skating, [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") and [Nordic skiing](/wiki/Nordic_skiing "Nordic skiing") were planned, but the 1916 Olympics was cancelled after the outbreak of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").[[14]](#cite_note-cbc.ca-17)

### 1920 to 1936

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Winter_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1920 to 1936")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Sonja_Henie_gold_1928.jpg/250px-Sonja_Henie_gold_1928.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sonja_Henie_gold_1928.jpg)

Sonia Henie's Olympic gold medal, St. Moritz 1928.

The first Olympics after the war, the [1920 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics"), were held in [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), Belgium,[[16]](#cite_note-Games1920-19) and featured figure skating[[17]](#cite_note-1920figureskating-20) and an ice hockey tournament. Germany, Austria, Hungary, Bulgaria and Turkey were banned from competing in the games. At the [IOC Congress](/wiki/List_of_IOC_meetings "List of IOC meetings") held the following year it was decided that the host nation of the [1924 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1924_Summer_Olympics "1924 Summer Olympics"), France, would host a separate "International Winter Sports Week" under the patronage of the IOC. [Chamonix](/wiki/Chamonix "Chamonix") was chosen to host this week (actually 11 days) of events.

[The 1924 games](/wiki/1924_Winter_Olympics "1924 Winter Olympics") in Chamonix proved to be a success when more than 250 athletes from 16 nations competed in 16 events.[[18]](#cite_note-Games1924-21) Athletes [from Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_1924_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 1924 Winter Olympics") [and Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1924_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1924 Winter Olympics") won 28 medals, more than the rest of the participating nations combined.[[19]](#cite_note-22) The first gold medal awarded was won by [Charles Jewtraw](/wiki/Charles_Jewtraw "Charles Jewtraw") of the United States in the 500-meter speed skate. [Sonja Henie](/wiki/Sonja_Henie "Sonja Henie") of Norway, at just 11 years old, competed in the ladies' figure skating and, although finishing last, became popular with fans. [Gillis Grafström](/wiki/Gillis_Grafstr%C3%B6m "Gillis Grafström") of Sweden defended his 1920 gold medal[[17]](#cite_note-1920figureskating-20) in men's figure skating, becoming the first Olympian to win gold medals in both Summer and Winter Olympics.[[20]](#cite_note-23) Germany remained banned until 1925, and instead hosted a series of games called [Deutsche Kampfspiele](/wiki/Deutsche_Kampfspiele "Deutsche Kampfspiele"), starting with the winter edition of 1922 (which predated the first Winter Olympics). In 1925 the IOC decided to create a separate winter event and the 1924 games in Chamonix were retroactively designated as the first Winter Olympics.[[14]](#cite_note-cbc.ca-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Games1924-21)

[St. Moritz](/wiki/St._Moritz "St. Moritz"), Switzerland, was appointed by the IOC to host the [second Winter Games](/wiki/1928_Winter_Olympics "1928 Winter Olympics") in 1928.[[21]](#cite_note-24) Fluctuating weather conditions challenged the hosts. The [opening ceremony](/wiki/Olympic_Games_ceremony "Olympic Games ceremony") was held in a blizzard while warm weather conditions plagued sporting events throughout the rest of the games.[[22]](#cite_note-25) Because of the weather the 10,000 metre speed-skating event had to be abandoned and officially cancelled.[[23]](#cite_note-warmweather-26) The weather was not the only noteworthy aspect of the 1928 games: [Sonja Henie](/wiki/Sonja_Henie "Sonja Henie") of [Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1928_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1928 Winter Olympics") returned to the Winter Olympics to make history when she won the [ladies' figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1928_Winter_Olympics "Figure skating at the 1928 Winter Olympics") at the age of 15. She became the youngest Olympic champion in history, a distinction she held for 70 years, and went on to defend her title at the next two Winter Olympics.[[24]](#cite_note-Games1928-27) [Gillis Grafström](/wiki/Gillis_Grafstr%C3%B6m "Gillis Grafström") won his third consecutive figure skating gold[[25]](#cite_note-28) and went on to win silver in 1932,[[26]](#cite_note-1932Figureskating-29) becoming the most decorated men's figure skater to date.

The [next Winter Olympics](/wiki/1932_Winter_Olympics "1932 Winter Olympics"), held in Lake Placid, New York, United States was the first to be hosted outside of Europe. Seventeen nations and 252 athletes participated.[[27]](#cite_note-Games1932-30) This was less than in 1928, as the journey to [Lake Placid](/wiki/Lake_Placid,_New_York "Lake Placid, New York") was too long and expensive for some European nations that encountered financial problems in the midst of the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"). The athletes competed in fourteen events in four sports.[[27]](#cite_note-Games1932-30) Virtually no snow fell for two months before the Games, and there was not enough snow to hold all the events until mid-January.[[28]](#cite_note-31) Sonja Henie defended her Olympic title,[[26]](#cite_note-1932Figureskating-29) and [Eddie Eagan](/wiki/Eddie_Eagan "Eddie Eagan") of the United States, who had been an Olympic champion in boxing in 1920,[[29]](#cite_note-32) won the gold medal in the [men's bobsleigh event](/wiki/Bobsleigh_at_the_1932_Winter_Olympics "Bobsleigh at the 1932 Winter Olympics")[[30]](#cite_note-33) to join [Gillis Grafström](/wiki/Gillis_Grafstr%C3%B6m "Gillis Grafström") as the only athletes to have won gold medals in both the [Summer](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games") and Winter Olympics.[[27]](#cite_note-Games1932-30) Eagan has the distinction as the only Olympian as of 2020 to accomplish this feat in different sports.[[31]](#cite_note-34)

The German towns of [Garmisch and Partenkirchen](/wiki/Garmisch-Partenkirchen "Garmisch-Partenkirchen") joined to organise the [1936 Winter Games](/wiki/1936_Winter_Olympics "1936 Winter Olympics"), held from 6–16 February.[[32]](#cite_note-35) This was the last time the [Summer](/wiki/1936_Summer_Olympics "1936 Summer Olympics") and Winter Olympics were held in the same country in the same year. [Alpine skiing](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1936_Winter_Olympics "Alpine skiing at the 1936 Winter Olympics") made its Olympic debut, but skiing teachers were barred from entering because they were considered to be professionals.[[33]](#cite_note-Games1936-36) Because of this decision [the Swiss](/wiki/Switzerland_at_the_1936_Winter_Olympics "Switzerland at the 1936 Winter Olympics") [and Austrian](/wiki/Austria_at_the_1936_Winter_Olympics "Austria at the 1936 Winter Olympics") skiers refused to compete at the games.[[33]](#cite_note-Games1936-36)

[World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") interrupted the Winter Olympics. The [1940 games](/wiki/1940_Winter_Olympics "1940 Winter Olympics") had been awarded to [Sapporo](/wiki/Sapporo "Sapporo"), Japan, but the decision was rescinded in 1938 because of the Japanese [invasion](/wiki/Sino-Japanese_War_(1937%E2%80%931945) "Sino-Japanese War (1937–1945)") of China. The games were then to be held at Garmisch-Partenkirchen, Germany, but the 1940 games were cancelled following the German [invasion of Poland](/wiki/Invasion_of_Poland "Invasion of Poland") in 1939.[[34]](#cite_note-37) Due to the ongoing war, the [1944 games](/wiki/1944_Winter_Olympics "1944 Winter Olympics"), originally scheduled for [Cortina D'Ampezzo](/wiki/Cortina_D%27Ampezzo "Cortina D'Ampezzo"), Italy, were cancelled.[[35]](#cite_note-38)

### 1948 to 1962

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Winter_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1948 to 1962")]

[![An open-air arena with crowds in the stands and people on the playing field surrounding a rostrum](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/1956_Winter_Olympics_opening_ceremonies.jpg/250px-1956_Winter_Olympics_opening_ceremonies.jpg)](/wiki/File:1956_Winter_Olympics_opening_ceremonies.jpg)

The opening ceremonies of the 1956 Winter Olympics in Cortina d'Ampezzo

St. Moritz was selected to host the [first post-war games, in 1948](/wiki/1948_Winter_Olympics "1948 Winter Olympics"). Switzerland's neutrality had protected the town during World War II, and most venues from the 1928 games remained in place, which made St. Moritz a logical choice. It became the first city to host a Winter Olympics twice.[[36]](#cite_note-39) Twenty-eight countries competed in Switzerland, but athletes from Germany and Japan were not invited because their Olympic committees were not yet recognized. They returned in 1952.[[37]](#cite_note-Games1948-40) Some controversy emerged when two hockey teams from the [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1948_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1948 Winter Olympics") arrived, both claiming to be the legitimate U.S. Olympic hockey representative. The [Olympic flag](/wiki/Olympic_flag#Flag "Olympic flag") presented at the [1920 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics") in Antwerp was stolen, as was its replacement. There was unprecedented parity at these games, during which 10 countries won gold medals—more than any games to that point.[[38]](#cite_note-41)

The [Olympic Flame](/wiki/Olympic_Flame "Olympic Flame") tradition was [introduced at the 1952 games](/wiki/1952_Winter_Olympics "1952 Winter Olympics") in [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo"): the flame was lit in the fireplace of the house of 19th-century Norwegian skiing pioneer [Sondre Nordheim](/wiki/Sondre_Nordheim "Sondre Nordheim") and the first Winter torch relay was conducted by 94 torchbearers entirely on their skis.[[39]](#cite_note-Games1952-42)[[40]](#cite_note-43) [Bandy](/wiki/Bandy "Bandy"), a popular sport in the Nordic countries, was featured as a demonstration sport, though [only Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1952_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1952 Winter Olympics"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_1952_Winter_Olympics "Sweden at the 1952 Winter Olympics"), [and Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_1952_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 1952 Winter Olympics") fielded teams. Norwegian athletes won 17 medals, which outpaced all the other nations.[[41]](#cite_note-44) They were led by [Hjalmar Andersen](/wiki/Hjalmar_Andersen "Hjalmar Andersen") who won three gold medals in four events in the [speed skating](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1952_Winter_Olympics "Speed skating at the 1952 Winter Olympics") competition.[[42]](#cite_note-45)

After not being able to host the games in 1944, [Cortina d'Ampezzo](/wiki/Cortina_d%27Ampezzo "Cortina d'Ampezzo") was selected to organise the [1956 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1956_Winter_Olympics "1956 Winter Olympics"). At the opening ceremonies the final torchbearer, Guido Caroli, entered the [Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Stadio_olimpico_del_ghiaccio "Stadio olimpico del ghiaccio") on ice skates. As he skated around the stadium his skate caught on a cable and he fell and burned his arm, nearly extinguishing the flame. He was able to recover and light the cauldron.[[43]](#cite_note-46) These were the first Winter Games to be televised, and the first Olympics ever broadcast to an international audience, though no television rights were sold until the [1960 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1960_Summer_Olympics "1960 Summer Olympics") in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome").[[44]](#cite_note-g136-47) The Cortina games were used to test the feasibility of televising large sporting events.[[44]](#cite_note-g136-47)

The [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_1956_Winter_Olympics "Soviet Union at the 1956 Winter Olympics") made its Olympic debut and had an immediate impact, winning more medals than any other nation.[[45]](#cite_note-Games1956-48) The Soviets' immediate success might be explained by the advent of the state-sponsored "full-time amateur athlete". The [USSR](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_Olympics "Soviet Union at the Olympics") entered teams of athletes who were all nominally students, soldiers, or working in a profession, but many of whom were in reality paid by the state to train full-time.[[46]](#cite_note-Benjamin_Daniel-49)[[47]](#cite_note-Otto_Schantz-50) [Chiharu Igaya](/wiki/Chiharu_Igaya "Chiharu Igaya") won the first Winter Olympics medal [for Japan](/wiki/Japan_at_the_1956_Winter_Olympics "Japan at the 1956 Winter Olympics") and the continent of Asia when he placed second in the slalom.[[48]](#cite_note-51)

The IOC awarded the [1960 Olympics](/wiki/1960_Winter_Olympics "1960 Winter Olympics") to [Squaw Valley](/wiki/Squaw_Valley_Ski_Resort "Squaw Valley Ski Resort"), United States.[[49]](#cite_note-Games1960-52) The announcement of its selection came as a shock as the resort near [Lake Tahoe](/wiki/Lake_Tahoe "Lake Tahoe") was undeveloped and unknown outside the United States. About US$80,000,000 was spent over four years to build cutting-edge infrastructure from scratch. The layout was designed to be intimate, allowing spectators and competitors to reach most of the venues on foot.[[50]](#cite_note-j27-53)[[51]](#cite_note-54) The opening and closing ceremonies were the firsts produced by [Walt Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney "Walt Disney") Company.[[52]](#cite_note-j28-55) The Squaw Valley Olympics was the first Winter Games to have a dedicated athletes' village,[[53]](#cite_note-56)[[54]](#cite_note-57) the first to use a computer (courtesy of [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM")) to tabulate results, and the first to feature female speed skating events. This edition is the only one to date to not have bobsleigh competitions, as the number of countries registered in the event was insufficient and the costs of building the track were too high for the Organizing Committee. To replace the event, an extra edition from the [FIBT World Championship was held](/wiki/FIBT_World_Championships_1960 "FIBT World Championships 1960").[[52]](#cite_note-j28-55)

### 1964 to 1980

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Winter_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1964 to 1980")]

[![An empty arena with the sheet of ice and the score board](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Miracle_on_Ice.jpg/250px-Miracle_on_Ice.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miracle_on_Ice.jpg)

The [Herb Brooks Arena](/wiki/Herb_Brooks_Arena "Herb Brooks Arena")   
in [Lake Placid](/wiki/Lake_Placid,_New_York "Lake Placid, New York") (c. 2007), site of  
the "[Miracle on Ice](/wiki/Miracle_on_Ice "Miracle on Ice")" in [1980](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1980_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1980 Winter Olympics")

The Austrian city of [Innsbruck](/wiki/Innsbruck "Innsbruck") was the host in [1964](/wiki/1964_Winter_Olympics "1964 Winter Olympics"). For the first time, the Olympic torch of the Winter Olympic Games was lit at the traditional ritual held in the temple of Olympia. Although Innsbruck was a traditional winter sports resort, unprojected warm weather caused a lack of snow and organisers were unable to save enough snow to be used during the Games, and the Austrian army was enlisted to transport snow and ice from other places to the sports venues.[[52]](#cite_note-j28-55) [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_1964_Winter_Olympics "Soviet Union at the 1964 Winter Olympics") speed-skater [Lidia Skoblikova](/wiki/Lidia_Skoblikova "Lidia Skoblikova") made history by winning all four-speed skating events. Her career total of six gold medals set a record for Winter Olympics athletes.[[52]](#cite_note-j28-55) Also, for the first time [Luge](/wiki/Luge "Luge") was added to the Olympic program, but the sport received bad publicity when a competitor was killed in a pre-Olympic training run.[[55]](#cite_note-Games1964-58)[[56]](#cite_note-59)

Held in the French town of [Grenoble](/wiki/Grenoble "Grenoble"), the [1968 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1968_Winter_Olympics "1968 Winter Olympics") were the first Olympic Games to be broadcast in colour. There were 1,158 athletes from 37 nations competing in 35 events.[[57]](#cite_note-Games1968-60) [French](/wiki/France_at_the_1968_Winter_Olympics "France at the 1968 Winter Olympics") alpine ski racer [Jean-Claude Killy](/wiki/Jean-Claude_Killy "Jean-Claude Killy") became only the second person to win all the men's alpine skiing events. Due to the high interest around the world, the organising committee sold television rights for US$2 million, which was more than twice the cost of the broadcast rights for the Innsbruck Games.[[58]](#cite_note-fp277-61) For the first time, the organizers chose to decentralize the Games to save costs and the events were spread across three long distances clusters, which led to the need to build three Olympic Villages. Along with the high costs, the organisers claimed that this was necessary to accommodate technological advances, however, critics disputed this, alleging that the layout would incorporate the best possible venues for television broadcasts at the athletes' expense.[[58]](#cite_note-fp277-61)

The [1972 Winter Games](/wiki/1972_Winter_Olympics "1972 Winter Olympics"), held in [Sapporo](/wiki/Sapporo "Sapporo"), Japan,[[59]](#cite_note-Games1972-62) were the first to be hosted on a continent other than North America or Europe. The issue of professionalism was disputed during these Games when a number of alpine skiers were found to have participated in a ski camp at [Mammoth Mountain](/wiki/Mammoth_Mountain "Mammoth Mountain") in the United States; three days before the opening ceremony, IOC president [Avery Brundage](/wiki/Avery_Brundage "Avery Brundage") threatened to bar the skiers from competing in the Games as he insisted that they were no longer amateurs having benefited financially from their status as athletes.[[60]](#cite_note-63) Eventually only [Austrian](/wiki/Austria_at_the_1972_Winter_Olympics "Austria at the 1972 Winter Olympics") [Karl Schranz](/wiki/Karl_Schranz "Karl Schranz"), who earned more than the other skiers, was excluded from the competition.[[61]](#cite_note-64) Canada boycotted the [1972](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1972_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1972 Winter Olympics") and the [1976 ice hockey tournaments](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1976_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 1976 Winter Olympics") in protest at not being able to use players from professional leagues.[[62]](#cite_note-Num17-65) Canadian authorities also accused the Soviet Union of using state-sponsored athletes, who were de facto professionals.[[63]](#cite_note-66) [Francisco Fernández Ochoa](/wiki/Francisco_Fern%C3%A1ndez_Ochoa "Francisco Fernández Ochoa") became the first and, as of 2022, the only [Spaniard](/wiki/Spain_at_the_1972_Winter_Olympics "Spain at the 1972 Winter Olympics") to win a Winter Olympic gold medal when he triumphed in the [slalom](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1972_Winter_Olympics "Alpine skiing at the 1972 Winter Olympics").[[64]](#cite_note-factsheetp5-67)

The [1976 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1976_Winter_Olympics "1976 Winter Olympics") had initially been awarded in 1970 to [Denver](/wiki/Denver "Denver"), [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado") in the United States. These Games would have coincided with the year of Colorado's [centennial](/wiki/Centennial "Centennial") and the [United States Bicentennial](/wiki/United_States_Bicentennial "United States Bicentennial"). However, the increasing costs of the event and the oil crisis led to a local plebiscite held in November 1972, that resulted in the city withdrawing from hosting the Games, as the people of Colorado voted against public funding of the Games by a 3:2 margin.[[65]](#cite_note-68)[[66]](#cite_note-69) The IOC responded by offering the Games to [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver")-[Garibaldi](/wiki/Garibaldi_Ranges "Garibaldi Ranges"), [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia") in [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), which had previously been a finalist bid for the 1976 Games. However, a change in the provincial government resulted in an administration that did not support the Olympic bid, so the IOC's offer was rejected.[[67]](#cite_note-Games1976-70)

[Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City"), previously a candidate for the 1972 Winter Olympics, then put itself forward, but a tense political situation led IOC to invite Innsbruck to host the 1976 Games, as all the infrastructure used during the 1964 Games had been maintained. Despite only having half the usual time to prepare for the Games, Innsbruck accepted the invitation to replace Denver in February 1973.[[67]](#cite_note-Games1976-70) During the opening ceremonies, two cauldrons were lit because it was the second time that the Austrian town had hosted the Winter Games.[[67]](#cite_note-Games1976-70) The 1976 Games featured the first [combination bobsleigh-and-luge track](/wiki/Igls_bobsleigh,_luge,_and_skeleton_track "Igls bobsleigh, luge, and skeleton track"), in neighbouring [Igls](/wiki/Igls "Igls").[[64]](#cite_note-factsheetp5-67) The [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_1976_Winter_Olympics "Soviet Union at the 1976 Winter Olympics") won its fourth consecutive ice hockey gold medal.[[67]](#cite_note-Games1976-70)

[In 1980](/wiki/1980_Winter_Olympics "1980 Winter Olympics") the Winter Olympics returned to Lake Placid, which had hosted the 1932 Games. Unlike previous editions, Lake Placid had no competitors in this bid process. [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") made their Olympic debut at the games. The People's Republic of China and [Costa Rica](/wiki/Costa_Rica "Costa Rica"), the first tropical nation, competed for the first time at the Winter Games. The [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China "Republic of China") boycotted the Games, in protest of the IOC's recognition of the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") as the only "China", and its request for the Republic of China to compete as "[Chinese Taipei](/wiki/Chinese_Taipei "Chinese Taipei")". The PRC, on the other hand, returned to the Olympics for the first time since 1952 and made its Winter Olympic debut.[[68]](#cite_note-71)[[69]](#cite_note-Findling_and_Pelle_1996_p._299-72)

[American](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1980_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1980 Winter Olympics") speed-skater [Eric Heiden](/wiki/Eric_Heiden "Eric Heiden") set either an Olympic or World record in every one of the five [events](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1980_Winter_Olympics "Speed skating at the 1980 Winter Olympics") in which he competed, winning a total of five individual gold medals and breaking the record for most individual golds in a single Olympics (both Summer and Winter).[[70]](#cite_note-73) [Hanni Wenzel](/wiki/Hanni_Wenzel "Hanni Wenzel") won both the slalom and giant slalom and her country, [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein_at_the_1980_Winter_Olympics "Liechtenstein at the 1980 Winter Olympics"), became the smallest nation to produce an Olympic gold medallist.[[71]](#cite_note-Games1980-74) In the "[Miracle on Ice](/wiki/Miracle_on_Ice "Miracle on Ice")", the [American hockey team](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "United States men's national ice hockey team") composed of college players beat the favoured seasoned professionals from the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_national_ice_hockey_team "Soviet Union national ice hockey team"), and progressed to eventually win the gold medal.[[72]](#cite_note-75)[[d]](#cite_note-IOC1980-USA-Finland01-77)

### 1984 to 1998

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Winter_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1984 to 1998")]

[![Alberto Tomba in hat and ski clothes](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Alberto_Tomba_Zagreb_2009.jpg/250px-Alberto_Tomba_Zagreb_2009.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alberto_Tomba_Zagreb_2009.jpg)

[Alberto Tomba](/wiki/Alberto_Tomba "Alberto Tomba"), winner of five Olympic medals in Calgary, Albertville and Lillehammer

[Sapporo](/wiki/Sapporo "Sapporo"), Japan, and [Gothenburg](/wiki/Gothenburg "Gothenburg"), Sweden, were front-runners to host the [1984 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1984_Winter_Olympics "1984 Winter Olympics"). It was therefore a surprise when [Sarajevo](/wiki/Sarajevo "Sarajevo"), Yugoslavia, was selected as host.[[74]](#cite_note-sarajevosurprise-78) The Games were well-organised and not affected by the run-up to the [war](/wiki/Bosnian_War "Bosnian War") that engulfed the country eight years later.[[75]](#cite_note-Games1984-79) A total of 49 nations and 1,272 athletes participated in 39 events. Host nation Yugoslavia won its first Olympic medal when alpine skier [Jure Franko](/wiki/Jure_Franko "Jure Franko") won silver in the giant slalom. Another sporting highlight was the free dance performance of [British](/wiki/Great_Britain_at_the_1984_Winter_Olympics "Great Britain at the 1984 Winter Olympics") ice dancers [Jayne Torvill](/wiki/Jayne_Torvill "Jayne Torvill") and [Christopher Dean](/wiki/Christopher_Dean "Christopher Dean"); their *[Boléro](/wiki/Bol%C3%A9ro "Boléro")* routine received unanimous perfect scores for artistic impression, earning them the gold medal.[[75]](#cite_note-Games1984-79)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Olympic_Torch_Calgary_1988.jpg/250px-Olympic_Torch_Calgary_1988.jpg)](/wiki/File:Olympic_Torch_Calgary_1988.jpg)

The Olympic Torch from the [1988 Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/1988_Winter_Olympic_Games "1988 Winter Olympic Games") in [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary")

In 1988, the Canadian city of [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary") hosted the [first Winter Olympics](/wiki/1988_Winter_Olympics "1988 Winter Olympics") to span three weekends, lasting for a total of 16 days.[[76]](#cite_note-Games1988-80) New events were added in ski-jumping and speed skating, while future Olympic sports curling, [short track speed skating](/wiki/Short_track_speed_skating "Short track speed skating") and [freestyle skiing](/wiki/Freestyle_skiing "Freestyle skiing") made their debut appearance as demonstration sports. The speed skating events were held indoors for the first time, on the [Olympic Oval](/wiki/Olympic_Oval "Olympic Oval"). Dutch skater [Yvonne van Gennip](/wiki/Yvonne_van_Gennip "Yvonne van Gennip") won three gold medals and set two world records, beating skaters from the favoured [East German team](/wiki/East_Germany_at_the_1988_Winter_Olympics "East Germany at the 1988 Winter Olympics") in every race.[[77]](#cite_note-81)

Her medal total was equalled by [Finnish](/wiki/Finland_at_the_1988_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 1988 Winter Olympics") ski jumper [Matti Nykänen](/wiki/Matti_Nyk%C3%A4nen "Matti Nykänen"), who won all three events in his sport. [Alberto Tomba](/wiki/Alberto_Tomba "Alberto Tomba"), [an Italian](/wiki/Italy_at_the_1988_Winter_Olympics "Italy at the 1988 Winter Olympics") skier, made his Olympic debut by winning both the giant slalom and slalom. East German [Christa Rothenburger](/wiki/Christa_Luding-Rothenburger "Christa Luding-Rothenburger") won the women's 1,000 metre speed skating event. Seven months later she would earn a silver in track cycling at the [Summer Games](/wiki/1988_Summer_Olympics "1988 Summer Olympics") in [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul"), to become the only athlete to win medals in both a Summer and Winter Olympics in the same year.[[76]](#cite_note-Games1988-80) The 1988 games are well remembered in popular culture from two films based on its events: *[Cool Runnings](/wiki/Cool_Runnings "Cool Runnings")* about the [Jamaican bobsled team](/wiki/Jamaica_national_bobsled_team "Jamaica national bobsled team"); and *[Eddie the Eagle](/wiki/Eddie_the_Eagle_(film) "Eddie the Eagle (film)")* about British ski jumper [Michael Edwards](/wiki/Eddie_the_Eagle "Eddie the Eagle"), who finished last but set a British record of 73.5 metres.

The [1992 Winter Games](/wiki/1992_Winter_Olympics "1992 Winter Olympics") were the last to be held in the same year as the [Summer Games](/wiki/1992_Summer_Olympics "1992 Summer Olympics").[[78]](#cite_note-Games1992-82) They were hosted in the French [Savoie](/wiki/Savoie "Savoie") region, with 18 events held in the city of [Albertville](/wiki/Albertville "Albertville") and the remaining events spread out over the Savoie.[[78]](#cite_note-Games1992-82) Political changes of the time were reflected in the composition of the Olympic teams competing in France: this was the first Games to be held after the [fall of Communism](/wiki/Fall_of_Communism "Fall of Communism") and the [fall of the Berlin Wall](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Berlin_Wall "Fall of the Berlin Wall"), and Germany competed as a single nation for the first time since the 1964 Games.[[79]](#cite_note-Findling_and_Pelle_2004,_p._400-83)

Former [Yugoslavian](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia") republics [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia_at_the_1992_Winter_Olympics "Croatia at the 1992 Winter Olympics") and [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia_at_the_1992_Winter_Olympics "Slovenia at the 1992 Winter Olympics") made their debuts as independent nations; most of the former [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") republics still competed as a single team known as the [Unified Team](/wiki/Unified_Team_at_the_1992_Winter_Olympics "Unified Team at the 1992 Winter Olympics"), but the [Baltic States](/wiki/Baltic_States "Baltic States") made independent appearances for the first time since before World War II.[[79]](#cite_note-Findling_and_Pelle_2004,_p._400-83) At 16 years old, [Finnish](/wiki/Finland_at_the_1992_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 1992 Winter Olympics") ski jumper [Toni Nieminen](/wiki/Toni_Nieminen "Toni Nieminen") made history by becoming the youngest male Winter Olympic champion.[[80]](#cite_note-84) [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_at_the_1992_Winter_Olympics "New Zealand at the 1992 Winter Olympics") skier [Annelise Coberger](/wiki/Annelise_Coberger "Annelise Coberger") became the first Winter Olympic medallist from the southern hemisphere when she won a silver medal in the women's slalom.

The [1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics "1994 Winter Olympics"), held in [Lillehammer](/wiki/Lillehammer "Lillehammer"), Norway, were the first Winter Games to be held in a different year from the Summer Games. This change resulted from the decision reached in the [91st IOC Session (1986)](/wiki/List_of_IOC_meetings#IOC_Sessions "List of IOC meetings") to separate the Summer and Winter Games and place them in alternating even-numbered years.[[81]](#cite_note-Games1994-85) This allowed the IOC to space out the logistical and financial costs of the two events from one another, and generate advertising revenue from the Games every two years instead of four.[[82]](#cite_note-86) Lillehammer is the northernmost city to ever host the Winter Games. It was the second time the Games were held in Norway, after the [1952 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1952_Winter_Olympics "1952 Winter Olympics") in [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo"), and the first time the [Olympic Truce](/wiki/Olympic_Truce "Olympic Truce") was observed. As a result, after the [dissolution of Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Dissolution_of_Czechoslovakia "Dissolution of Czechoslovakia") in 1993, the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Czech Republic at the 1994 Winter Olympics") and [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Slovakia at the 1994 Winter Olympics") made their Olympic debuts.[[83]](#cite_note-87)

The women's figure skating competition drew media attention when [American skater](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1994 Winter Olympics") [Nancy Kerrigan](/wiki/Nancy_Kerrigan "Nancy Kerrigan") was injured on 6 January 1994, in an assault planned by the ex-husband of opponent [Tonya Harding](/wiki/Tonya_Harding "Tonya Harding").[[84]](#cite_note-88) Both skaters competed in the Games, but the gold medal was narrowly won by [Oksana Baiul](/wiki/Oksana_Baiul "Oksana Baiul") who became [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Ukraine at the 1994 Winter Olympics")'s first Olympic champion, while Kerrigan won the silver medal.[[85]](#cite_note-89)[[86]](#cite_note-90) [Johann Olav Koss](/wiki/Johann_Olav_Koss "Johann Olav Koss") of [Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1994 Winter Olympics") won three gold medals, coming first in all of the distance speed skating events.[[87]](#cite_note-91)

13-year-old [Kim Yoon-Mi](/wiki/Kim_Yun-Mi_(speed_skater) "Kim Yun-Mi (speed skater)") became the youngest-ever Olympic gold medallist when South Korea won the women's 3,000-metre speed skating relay. [Bjørn Dæhli](/wiki/Bj%C3%B8rn_D%C3%A6hli "Bjørn Dæhli") of Norway won a medal in four out of five cross-country events, becoming the most decorated Winter Olympian until then. [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_1994_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 1994 Winter Olympics") won the most events, with eleven gold medals, while Norway achieved 26 podium finishes, collecting the most medals overall on home ground. [Juan Antonio Samaranch](/wiki/Juan_Antonio_Samaranch "Juan Antonio Samaranch") described Lillehammer as "the best Olympic Winter Games ever" in his closing ceremony speech.[[88]](#cite_note-92)

The [1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics") were held in the Japanese city of [Nagano](/wiki/Nagano_(city) "Nagano (city)") and were the first Games to host more than 2,000 athletes.[[89]](#cite_note-Games1998-93) The [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") allowed its players to participate in the men's [ice hockey tournament](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Ice hockey at the 1998 Winter Olympics – Men's tournament") for the first time, and the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic_men%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Czech Republic men's national ice hockey team") won the tournament. Women's ice hockey made its debut, and the [United States](/wiki/United_States_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "United States women's national ice hockey team") won the gold medal.[[90]](#cite_note-94) [Bjørn Dæhlie](/wiki/Bj%C3%B8rn_D%C3%A6hlie "Bjørn Dæhlie") of [Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1998 Winter Olympics") won three gold medals in Nordic skiing, becoming the most decorated Winter Olympic athlete, with eight gold medals and twelve medals overall.[[89]](#cite_note-Games1998-93)[[91]](#cite_note-95) [Austrian](/wiki/Austria_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Austria at the 1998 Winter Olympics") [Hermann Maier](/wiki/Hermann_Maier "Hermann Maier") survived a crash during the downhill competition and returned to win gold in the [super-G](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_super-G "Alpine skiing at the 1998 Winter Olympics – Men's super-G") and the giant slalom.[[89]](#cite_note-Games1998-93) [Tara Lipinski](/wiki/Tara_Lipinski "Tara Lipinski") of the [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), aged just 15, became the youngest ever female gold medallist in an individual event when she won the [Ladies' Singles](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics#Ladies "Figure skating at the 1998 Winter Olympics"), a record that had stood since [Sonja Henie](/wiki/Sonja_Henie "Sonja Henie") of [Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1928_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1928 Winter Olympics") won the same event, also aged 15, in [St. Moritz in 1928](/wiki/1928_Winter_Olympics "1928 Winter Olympics"). New world records were set in [speed skating](/wiki/Speed_skating_at_the_1998_Winter_Olympics "Speed skating at the 1998 Winter Olympics") largely due to the introduction of the [clap skate](/wiki/Clap_skate "Clap skate").[[92]](#cite_note-96)

### 2002 to 2026

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Winter_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2002 to 2026")]

[![Interwoven steel frame several stories high with the lit flame at the top](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/2002_Winter_Olympics_flame.jpg/250px-2002_Winter_Olympics_flame.jpg)](/wiki/File:2002_Winter_Olympics_flame.jpg)

The Olympic flame during the Opening Ceremony of the 2002 Games in Salt Lake City

[After a tumultuous host city process](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympic_bid_scandal "2002 Winter Olympic bid scandal"), the [2002 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics") were held in [Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City,_Utah "Salt Lake City, Utah"), United States. 2,399 athletes from 77 National Olympic Committees participated in 78 events in 7 sports.[[93]](#cite_note-Games2002-97) These Games were the first to take place since the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") of 2001, which meant a higher degree of security to avoid a terrorist attack. The opening ceremony saw signs of the [aftermath of the events of that day](/wiki/Aftermath_of_the_September_11_attacks "Aftermath of the September 11 attacks"), including the flag that flew at [Ground Zero](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_site "World Trade Center site"), and honour guards of NYPD and [FDNY](/wiki/New_York_City_Fire_Department "New York City Fire Department") members.[[94]](#cite_note-98)

[German](/wiki/Germany_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 2002 Winter Olympics") [Georg Hackl](/wiki/Georg_Hackl "Georg Hackl") won a silver in the singles luge, becoming the first athlete in Olympic history to win medals in the same individual event in five consecutive Olympics.[[93]](#cite_note-Games2002-97) [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 2002 Winter Olympics") achieved an unprecedented double by winning both the men's and women's [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Ice hockey at the 2002 Winter Olympics") gold medals.[[93]](#cite_note-Games2002-97) Canada became embroiled with [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 2002 Winter Olympics") in a [controversy](/wiki/2002_Olympic_Winter_Games_figure_skating_scandal "2002 Olympic Winter Games figure skating scandal") that involved the judging of the [pairs figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Figure skating at the 2002 Winter Olympics") competition. The Russian pair of [Yelena Berezhnaya](/wiki/Yelena_Berezhnaya "Yelena Berezhnaya") and [Anton Sikharulidze](/wiki/Anton_Sikharulidze "Anton Sikharulidze") competed against the Canadian pair of [Jamie Salé](/wiki/Jamie_Sal%C3%A9 "Jamie Salé") and [David Pelletier](/wiki/David_Pelletier "David Pelletier") for the gold medal.[[95]](#cite_note-Roberts-99)

The Canadians appeared to have skated well enough to win the competition, yet the Russians were awarded the gold. The [French](/wiki/France_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "France at the 2002 Winter Olympics") judge, [Marie-Reine Le Gougne](/wiki/Marie-Reine_Le_Gougne "Marie-Reine Le Gougne"), awarded the gold to the Russians. An investigation revealed that she had been pressured to give the gold to the Russian pair regardless of how they skated; in return, the Russian judge would look favourably on the French entrants in the ice dancing competition.[[95]](#cite_note-Roberts-99)

The IOC decided to award both pairs the gold medal in a second [medal ceremony](/wiki/Olympic_Games_ceremony "Olympic Games ceremony") held later in the Games.[[96]](#cite_note-100) [Australian](/wiki/Australia_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Australia at the 2002 Winter Olympics") [Steven Bradbury](/wiki/Steven_Bradbury_(speed_skater) "Steven Bradbury (speed skater)") became the first gold medallist from the southern hemisphere when he won the 1,000 metre short-track speed skating event.[[97]](#cite_note-101)

[![Close-up of the Olympic Flame during the 2006 Winter Olympics in Turin](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Braciere_Olimpico.jpg/250px-Braciere_Olimpico.jpg)](/wiki/File:Braciere_Olimpico.jpg)

A close-up of the Olympic Flame during the [2006 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics") in [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin")

The Italian city of [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin") hosted the [2006 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics"). It was the second time that Italy had hosted the Winter Olympic Games. [South Korean](/wiki/South_Korea_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics "South Korea at the 2006 Winter Olympics") athletes won 10 medals, including 6 gold in the short-track speed skating events. [Sun-Yu Jin](/wiki/Jin_Sun-Yu "Jin Sun-Yu") won three gold medals while her teammate [Hyun-Soo Ahn](/wiki/Hyun-Soo_Ahn "Hyun-Soo Ahn") won three gold medals and a bronze.[[98]](#cite_note-Games2006-102) In the women's [Cross-Country](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics "Cross-country skiing at the 2006 Winter Olympics") team pursuit [Canadian](/wiki/Canada_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 2006 Winter Olympics") [Sara Renner](/wiki/Sara_Renner "Sara Renner") broke one of her poles and, when he saw her dilemma, [Norwegian](/wiki/Norway_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 2006 Winter Olympics") coach [Bjørnar Håkensmoen](/wiki/Bj%C3%B8rnar_H%C3%A5kensmoen "Bjørnar Håkensmoen") decided to lend her a pole. In so doing she was able to help her team win a silver medal in the event at the expense of the Norwegian team, who finished fourth.[[98]](#cite_note-Games2006-102)[[99]](#cite_note-103)

On winning the Super-G, [Kjetil-Andre Aamodt](/wiki/Kjetil-Andre_Aamodt "Kjetil-Andre Aamodt") of Norway became the most decorated ski racer of all time with 4 gold and 8 overall medals. He is also the only ski racer to have won the same event at three Olympics, winning the Super-G in [1992](/wiki/1992_Winter_Olympics "1992 Winter Olympics"), [2002](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympics "2002 Winter Olympics"), and [2006](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics"). [Claudia Pechstein](/wiki/Claudia_Pechstein "Claudia Pechstein") of [Germany](/wiki/Germany_at_the_2006_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 2006 Winter Olympics") became the first speed skater to earn nine career medals.[[98]](#cite_note-Games2006-102)

In February 2009, Pechstein tested positive for ["blood manipulation"](/wiki/Blood_doping "Blood doping") and received a two-year suspension, which she appealed. The [Court of Arbitration for Sport](/wiki/Court_of_Arbitration_for_Sport "Court of Arbitration for Sport") upheld her suspension but a Swiss court ruled that she could compete for a spot on the [2010 German Olympic team](/wiki/Germany_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Germany at the 2010 Winter Olympics").[[100]](#cite_note-104) This ruling was brought to the Swiss Federal Tribunal, which overturned the lower court's ruling and precluded her from competing in Vancouver.[[101]](#cite_note-105)

In 2003, the IOC awarded the [2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics") to [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), thus allowing Canada to host its second Winter Olympics. With a population of more than 2.5 million people Vancouver is the largest metropolitan area to ever host a Winter Olympic Games.[[102]](#cite_note-106) Over 2,500 athletes from 82 countries participated in 86 events.[[103]](#cite_note-Games2010-107) The death of [Georgian](/wiki/Georgia_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Georgia at the 2010 Winter Olympics") luger [Nodar Kumaritashvili](/wiki/Nodar_Kumaritashvili "Nodar Kumaritashvili") in a training run on the day of the opening ceremonies resulted in the [Whistler Sliding Centre](/wiki/Whistler_Sliding_Centre "Whistler Sliding Centre") changing the track layout on safety grounds.[[104]](#cite_note-108)

[Norwegian](/wiki/Norway_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 2010 Winter Olympics") cross-country skier [Marit Bjørgen](/wiki/Marit_Bj%C3%B8rgen "Marit Bjørgen") won five medals in the six cross-country events on the women's programme. She finished the Olympics with three golds, a silver and a bronze.[[105]](#cite_note-109) For the first time, [Canada](/wiki/Canada_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the 2010 Winter Olympics") won a gold medal at an Olympic Games it hosted, having failed to do so at both the [1976 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics") in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") and the [1988 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1988_Winter_Olympics "1988 Winter Olympics") in [Calgary](/wiki/Calgary "Calgary"). In contrast to the lack of gold medals at these previous Olympics, the Canadian team finished first overall in gold medal wins,[[106]](#cite_note-clinch-110) and became the first host nation—since [Norway in 1952](/wiki/Norway_at_the_1952_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 1952 Winter Olympics")—to lead the gold medal count, with 14 medals. In doing so, it also broke the record for the most gold medals won by a NOC at a single Winter Olympics (the previous was 13, set by the [Soviet Union in 1976](/wiki/Soviet_Union_at_the_1976_Winter_Olympics "Soviet Union at the 1976 Winter Olympics") and matched by [Norway in 2002](/wiki/Norway_at_the_2002_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 2002 Winter Olympics")).[[107]](#cite_note-RecordGolds-111)

The Vancouver Games were notable for the poor performance of the [Russian](/wiki/Russia_at_the_2010_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 2010 Winter Olympics") athletes. From their first Winter Olympics in [1956](/wiki/1956_Winter_Olympics "1956 Winter Olympics") to the 2006 Games, a Soviet or Russian delegation had never been outside the top five medal-winning nations, but in 2010 they finished sixth in total medals and eleventh in gold medals. [President](/wiki/President_of_Russia "President of Russia") [Dmitry Medvedev](/wiki/Dmitry_Medvedev "Dmitry Medvedev") called for the resignation of top sports officials immediately after the Games.[[108]](#cite_note-112) Russia's disappointing performance at Vancouver is cited as the reason behind the [enhancement of an already existing doping scheme](/wiki/Doping_in_Russia "Doping in Russia") alleged to have been in operation at major events such as the 2014 Games at Sochi.[[109]](#cite_note-113)

The success of Asian countries stood in stark contrast to the under-performing Russian team, with Vancouver marking a high point for medals won by Asian countries. At the Albertville Games in 1992 the Asian countries had won fifteen medals, three of which were gold. In Vancouver, the total number of medals won by athletes from Asia had increased to thirty-one, with eleven of them being gold. The rise of Asian nations in Winter Olympics sports is due in part to the growth of winter sports programmes and the interest in winter sports in nations such as Kazakhstan, South Korea, Japan and China. These results increased the chances of an Asian city hosting the [2018 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics "2018 Winter Olympics") that would be held the following year.[[110]](#cite_note-114)[[111]](#cite_note-115)

[Sochi](/wiki/Sochi "Sochi"), Russia, was selected as the host city for the [2014 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2014_Winter_Olympics "2014 Winter Olympics") over [Salzburg](/wiki/Salzburg "Salzburg"), Austria, and [Pyeongchang](/wiki/Pyeongchang_County "Pyeongchang County"), South Korea. This was the first time that Russia had hosted a Winter Olympics.[[112]](#cite_note-Games2014-116) The Games took place from 7 to 23 February 2014.[[113]](#cite_note-117) A record 2,800 athletes from 88 countries competed in 98 events. The Olympic Village and Olympic Stadium were located on the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") coast. All of the mountain venues were 50 kilometres (31 mi) away in the alpine region known as [Krasnaya Polyana](/wiki/Krasnaya_Polyana,_Sochi,_Krasnodar_Krai "Krasnaya Polyana, Sochi, Krasnodar Krai").[[112]](#cite_note-Games2014-116) The Games were the most expensive until the date, with a cost of £30 billion (US$51 billion).

On the snow, Norwegian biathlete [Ole Einar Bjørndalen](/wiki/Ole_Einar_Bj%C3%B8rndalen "Ole Einar Bjørndalen") took two golds to bring his total tally of Olympic medals to 13, overtaking his compatriot Bjørn Dæhlie to become the most decorated Winter Olympian of all time. Another Norwegian, cross-country skier [Marit Bjørgen](/wiki/Marit_Bj%C3%B8rgen "Marit Bjørgen") took three golds; her total of ten Olympic medals tied her as the female Winter Olympian with most medals, alongside [Raisa Smetanina](/wiki/Raisa_Smetanina "Raisa Smetanina") and [Stefania Belmondo](/wiki/Stefania_Belmondo "Stefania Belmondo"). Snowboarder [Ayumu Hirano](/wiki/Ayumu_Hirano "Ayumu Hirano") became the youngest medallist on snow at the Winter Games when he took a silver in the [halfpipe](/wiki/Snowboarding_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_halfpipe "Snowboarding at the 2014 Winter Olympics – Men's halfpipe") competition at the age of fifteen.[[112]](#cite_note-Games2014-116)

On the ice, the Netherlands team dominated the speed skating events, taking 23 medals, four clean sweeps of the podium places and at least one medal in each of the twelve medal events. [Ireen Wüst](/wiki/Ireen_W%C3%BCst "Ireen Wüst") was their most successful competitor, taking two golds and three silvers. In figure skating, [Yuzuru Hanyu](/wiki/Yuzuru_Hanyu "Yuzuru Hanyu") became the first skater to break the 100-point barrier in the short programme on the way to winning the gold medal. Among the sledding disciplines, luger [Armin Zöggeler](/wiki/Armin_Z%C3%B6ggeler "Armin Zöggeler") took a bronze, becoming the first Winter Olympian to secure a medal in six consecutive Games.[[112]](#cite_note-Games2014-116)

Following their disappointing performance at the 2010 Games, and an investment of £600 million in elite sport, [Russia](/wiki/Russia_at_the_2014_Winter_Olympics "Russia at the 2014 Winter Olympics") initially topped the [medal table](/wiki/2014_Winter_Olympics_medal_table "2014 Winter Olympics medal table"), taking 13 gold and 33 total medals.[[114]](#cite_note-118) However, [Grigory Rodchenkov](/wiki/Grigory_Rodchenkov "Grigory Rodchenkov"), the former head of the Russian national anti-doping laboratory, subsequently claimed that he had been involved in doping dozens of Russian competitors for the Games, and that he had been assisted by the Russian [Federal Security Service](/wiki/Federal_Security_Service "Federal Security Service") in opening and re-sealing bottles containing urine samples so that samples with banned substances could be replaced with "clean" urine.[[115]](#cite_note-119)

A subsequent [investigation](/wiki/McLaren_Report "McLaren Report") commissioned by the [World Anti-Doping Agency](/wiki/World_Anti-Doping_Agency "World Anti-Doping Agency") led by [Richard McLaren](/wiki/Richard_McLaren_(academic) "Richard McLaren (academic)") concluded that a state-sponsored doping programme had operated in Russia from "at least late 2011 to 2015" across the "vast majority" of Summer and Winter Olympic sports.[[116]](#cite_note-120) On 5 December 2017, the IOC announced that [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Olympic_Committee "Russian Olympic Committee") would compete as the [Olympic Athletes from Russia](/wiki/Olympic_Athletes_from_Russia_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics "Olympic Athletes from Russia at the 2018 Winter Olympics") at the 2018 Winter Olympics[[117]](#cite_note-russianban-121) and by the end of 2017 the [IOC Disciplinary Commission](/wiki/Oswald_Commission "Oswald Commission") had disqualified 43 Russian athletes, stripping thirteen medals and knocking Russia from the top of the medal table, thus putting Norway in the lead.[[118]](#cite_note-122)[[119]](#cite_note-123)[[120]](#cite_note-124) However, nine medals were later returned, meaning that Russia reclaimed first place in the overall medal table, and joint first place with Norway in terms of gold medals.

On 6 July 2011, [Pyeongchang](/wiki/Pyeongchang_County "Pyeongchang County"), South Korea, was selected to host the [2018 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics "2018 Winter Olympics") over [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"), Germany, and [Annecy](/wiki/Annecy "Annecy"), France.[[121]](#cite_note-2018Bids-125) This was the first time that [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") had been selected to host a Winter Olympics and it was the second time the Olympics were held in the country overall, after the [1988 Summer Olympics in Seoul](/wiki/1988_Summer_Olympics "1988 Summer Olympics"). The Games took place from 9 to 25 February 2018.[[122]](#cite_note-Games2018-126) More than 2,900 athletes from 92 countries participated in 102 events. The main venue cluster was the [Alpensia Resort](/wiki/Alpensia_Resort "Alpensia Resort") in [Daegwallyeong-myeon](/wiki/Daegwallyeong-myeon "Daegwallyeong-myeon"), while the ice events are held at [Gangneung Olympic Park](/wiki/Gangneung_Olympic_Park "Gangneung Olympic Park") in Pyeongchang's neighbouring sea-city of [Gangneung](/wiki/Gangneung "Gangneung").

The lead-up to the 2018 Winter Olympics was affected by [the tensions between North and South Korea](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympics#North_Korean_relations "2018 Winter Olympics") and the ongoing [Russian doping scandal](/wiki/Doping_in_Russia "Doping in Russia"). Despite [tense relations](/wiki/North_Korea%E2%80%93South_Korea_relations "North Korea–South Korea relations"), [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea") agreed to participate in the Games, enter with South Korea during the opening ceremony as a [unified Korea](/wiki/Unified_Korea "Unified Korea"), and field a [unified team](/wiki/Korea_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics "Korea at the 2018 Winter Olympics") in [women's ice hockey](/wiki/Korea_women%27s_national_ice_hockey_team "Korea women's national ice hockey team"). Russian athletes, who complied with the IOC's doping regulations, were given the option to compete in Pyeongchang as "Olympic Athletes from Russia" (OAR).[[117]](#cite_note-russianban-121)

The Games saw the addition of [big air snowboarding](/wiki/Snowboarding#Big_air "Snowboarding"), [mass start](/wiki/Mass_start "Mass start") [speed skating](/wiki/Speed_skating "Speed skating"), [mixed doubles curling](/wiki/Mixed_doubles_curling "Mixed doubles curling"), and mixed team [alpine skiing](/wiki/Alpine_skiing "Alpine skiing") to the programme. Like four years early, the Netherlands again dominated speed skating, winning gold medals in seven of the ten individual events. Dutch speed skater [Sven Kramer](/wiki/Sven_Kramer "Sven Kramer") won gold in the men's 5000m event, becoming the only male speed skater to win the same Olympic event three times. On the snow, Norway led the medal tally in cross-country skiing, with [Marit Bjørgen](/wiki/Marit_Bj%C3%B8rgen "Marit Bjørgen") winning bronze in the women's team sprint and gold in the 30-kilometre classical event, bringing her total Olympic medal haul to fifteen, the most won by any athlete (male or female) in Winter Olympics history.

[Johannes Høsflot Klæbo](/wiki/Johannes_H%C3%B8sflot_Kl%C3%A6bo "Johannes Høsflot Klæbo") of Norway became the youngest ever male to win an Olympic gold in cross-country skiing when he won the [men's sprint](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_sprint "Cross-country skiing at the 2018 Winter Olympics – Men's sprint") at age 21. He was also part of the Norwegian cross-country team winning the men’s relay and team sprint events. [Noriaki Kasai](/wiki/Noriaki_Kasai "Noriaki Kasai") of Japan became the first athlete in history to participate in eight Winter Olympics when he took part in the ski jumping qualification the day before the opening of the Games. [Ester Ledecká](/wiki/Ester_Ledeck%C3%A1 "Ester Ledecká") of the Czech Republic won gold in the skiing super-G event and another gold in the snowboarding parallel giant slalom, making her the first female athlete to win Olympic gold medals in two sports at a single Winter Games.

Norway led the total medal standings with 39, the highest number of medals by a nation in any Winter Olympics, followed by Germany's 31 and Canada's 29. Host nation [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_at_the_2018_Winter_Olympics "South Korea at the 2018 Winter Olympics") won seventeen medals, five of them gold, its highest medal haul at a Winter Olympics.

[Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), the capital of the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China"), was elected as the host city for the [2022 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2022_Winter_Olympics "2022 Winter Olympics") on 31 July 2015 at the [128th IOC Session](/wiki/128th_IOC_Session "128th IOC Session"). Beijing became the first city ever to have hosted both the Summer and Winter Olympics. Like the Summer Olympics held six months earlier in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") resulted in the implementation of strict health and safety protocols, including restrictions on public attendance at the Games. The Games included a record 109 events over 15 disciplines in seven sports with seven new medal events, including mixed team competitions in freestyle skiing aerials, ski jumping, and snowboard cross. The Games were held between 4 and 20 February 2022 at venues in Beijing and [Zhangjiakou](/wiki/Zhangjiakou "Zhangjiakou") which for the first time were run entirely on [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy"). Several of the events were impacted by temperatures as low as minus 20 [Celsius](/wiki/Celsius "Celsius") and strong wind.

The first gold medal of the Games was won by [Therese Johaug](/wiki/Therese_Johaug "Therese Johaug") of [Norway](/wiki/Norway_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics "Norway at the 2022 Winter Olympics") in the women's skiathlon. Johaug had been excluded from the 2018 Winter Olympics in a controversial decision after having used a banned cream for sunburned lips. She went on to also win the women's 10 km and 30 km cross-country distances. Johannes Klæbo continued his dominance of the men’s cross-country sprint events by winning gold in the individual and team events. In the women's snowboard cross, [Lindsey Jacobellis](/wiki/Lindsey_Jacobellis "Lindsey Jacobellis") of the [United States](/wiki/United_States_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics "United States at the 2022 Winter Olympics") won the gold, having lost the gold 16 years earlier at the [2006 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics") in Torino due to a brutal fall. On the ice, the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics "Netherlands at the 2022 Winter Olympics") dominated with a total of six gold medals and [Irene Schouten](/wiki/Irene_Schouten "Irene Schouten") winning the women's mass start, 3,000m and 5,000m distances. [Nils van der Poel](/wiki/Nils_van_der_Poel "Nils van der Poel") of [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics "Sweden at the 2022 Winter Olympics") won the men's 5,000m and 10,000m distances, setting new Olympic records in both distances. [Kamila Valieva](/wiki/Kamila_Valieva "Kamila Valieva") of [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Olympic_Committee_athletes_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics "Russian Olympic Committee athletes at the 2022 Winter Olympics") was allowed to compete in the [women's figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_singles "Figure skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics – Women's singles") despite a failed doping test in December 2021. She failed, however, to win an individual medal after falling in her final routine. Russia's [team gold medal](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Team_event "Figure skating at the 2022 Winter Olympics – Team event") remained in limbo for two years, pending investigation into Valieva's positive drug test, before the [Court of Arbitration for Sport](/wiki/Court_of_Arbitration_for_Sport "Court of Arbitration for Sport") (CAS) disqualified Valieva for four years retroactive to 25 December 2021, leading the ISU to re-allocate the medals, upgrading the United States to gold and Japan to silver while downgrading the ROC to bronze.[[123]](#cite_note-127) [Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_2022_Winter_Olympics "Finland at the 2022 Winter Olympics") claimed its first ice hockey gold, having beaten the Russian Olympic Committee in the men's final on the last day of the Games.

Norway was first in the overall medal standings, claiming 37 medals in total and 16 gold medals, the highest number of gold medals of any country in a single Winter Olympics. This was the tenth time Norway claimed the highest number of gold medals at the Winter Games.

### Future

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Winter_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Future")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/2010-2070-2100_Climate_change_threatens_venues_of_Olympic_Winter_Games.svg/250px-2010-2070-2100_Climate_change_threatens_venues_of_Olympic_Winter_Games.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2010-2070-2100_Climate_change_threatens_venues_of_Olympic_Winter_Games.svg)

The amount by which greenhouse gas emissions are reduced is forecast to substantially affect the number of Winter Olympic Game venues that will have reliably snowy conditions.[[124]](#cite_note-Forbes_20220204-128)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/20260201_Reliability_of_venues_for_snow_-_Winter_Olympics.svg/250px-20260201_Reliability_of_venues_for_snow_-_Winter_Olympics.svg.png)](/wiki/File:20260201_Reliability_of_venues_for_snow_-_Winter_Olympics.svg)

Though Europe and North America have the most potential venues (areas of rectangles in chart), a higher portion of those venues are projected to be climatically unreliable because of global warming (height of columns).[[125]](#cite_note-CurrentIssuesTourism_20240913-129)

The [2026 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2026_Winter_Olympics "2026 Winter Olympics") was held in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan")-[Cortina d'Ampezzo](/wiki/Cortina_d%27Ampezzo "Cortina d'Ampezzo"), Italy, and took place from 6 to 22 February 2026.[[126]](#cite_note-Games2026-130) At the [142nd IOC Session](/wiki/142nd_IOC_Session "142nd IOC Session") in July 2024, it was confirmed that the [2030 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2030_Winter_Olympics "2030 Winter Olympics") will be hosted by France in the [French Alps](/wiki/French_Alps "French Alps").[[127]](#cite_note-131) The [2034 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2034_Winter_Olympics "2034 Winter Olympics") was also awarded to be hosted by the United States in [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"); the state previously hosted the 2002 Olympics in [Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City").[[128]](#cite_note-SLCtime-132)

### Controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Winter_Olympic_Games&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Controversy")]

Main article: [Olympic Games scandals and controversies § Winter Olympics](/wiki/Olympic_Games_scandals_and_controversies#Winter_Olympics "Olympic Games scandals and controversies")

[![a head shot of Juan Antonio Samaranch with dark glasses on](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Juan_Antonio_Samaranch_DF-ST-01-00128.jpg/250px-Juan_Antonio_Samaranch_DF-ST-01-00128.jpg)](/wiki/File:Juan_Antonio_Samaranch_DF-ST-01-00128.jpg)

[Juan Antonio Samaranch](/wiki/Juan_Antonio_Samaranch "Juan Antonio Samaranch"), former IOC president, who was in charge of the Olympic movement for more than 20 years

The process for awarding host city honours came under intense scrutiny after [Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City") had been awarded the right to host the 2002 Games.[[129]](#cite_note-slcorruption-133) Soon after the host city had been announced it was discovered that the organisers had engaged in an elaborate bribery [scheme](/wiki/2002_Winter_Olympic_bid_scandal "2002 Winter Olympic bid scandal") to curry favour with IOC officials.[[129]](#cite_note-slcorruption-133) Gifts and other financial considerations were given to those who would evaluate and vote on Salt Lake City's bid. These gifts included medical treatment for relatives, a college scholarship for one member's son and a land deal in Utah. Even IOC president [Juan Antonio Samaranch](/wiki/Juan_Antonio_Samaranch "Juan Antonio Samaranch") received two rifles valued at $2,000. Samaranch defended the gift as inconsequential since, as president, he was a non-voting member.[[130]](#cite_note-Cashmore-134)

The subsequent [United States Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") investigation uncovered inconsistencies in the bids for every Olympics (both Summer and Winter) since 1988.[[131]](#cite_note-135) For example, the gifts received by IOC members from the Japanese Organising Committee for Nagano's bid for the [1998 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1998_Winter_Olympics "1998 Winter Olympics") were described by the investigation committee as "astronomical".[[132]](#cite_note-136) Although nothing strictly illegal had been done, the IOC feared that corporate sponsors would lose faith in the integrity of the process and that the Olympic brand would be tarnished to such an extent that advertisers would begin to pull their support.[[133]](#cite_note-137)

The investigation resulted in the expulsion of 10 IOC members and the sanctioning of another 10.
